revisions.content: Capital of Japan.

'''Tokyo''' (東京) is the capital and largest city of [[Japan]]. 



The population of Tokyo is around 8 million, with a total of more than 12 million in the metropolitan area.



Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], the city was known as (江戸) "Edo." The [[Tokugawa]] [[shogunate]] was established in 1603 with Edo as its de facto capital. (Since the emperor's residence was in [[Kyoto]], that city was formally the capital of Japan.) In 1868, when the shogunate came to an end, the city was renamed "Tokyo"; during the restoration, the emperor moved to Tokyo, making the city the formal as well as de facto capital of Japan.



A major [[earthquake]] struck Tokyo in 1923, killing approximately 70,000 people; a massive reconstruction plan was drawn up, but was too expensive to carry out except in part. Despite this, the city grew until the beginning of [[World War II]]. During the war, Tokyo was bombed heavily, and its population in 1945 was only half that of 1940.



After the war, Tokyo recovered and continued to grow, though the current [[recession]] affecting Japan has hurt the city.



Famous parts of the city and other sights include:

*[[Akihabara]]

*[[Ginza]]

*[[Asakusa]]

[[fr:Tokyo]][[eo:Tokio]][[de:Tokio]]
'''Tokyo''' (東京) is the capital and largest city of [[Japan]]. It is the capital of Tokyo prefecture and is located next to Tokyo Bay at the heart of the [[Kanto]] region.

The population of Tokyo is around 8 million, with a total of more than 12 million in the metropolitan area.

Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], the city was known as (江戸) "Edo." The [[Tokugawa]] [[shogunate]] was established in [[1603]] with Edo as its de facto capital. (Since the emperor's residence was in [[Kyoto]], that city was formally the capital of Japan.) In [[1868]], when the shogunate came to an end, the city was renamed "Tokyo"; during the restoration, the emperor moved to Tokyo, making the city the formal as well as de facto capital of Japan.

A major [[earthquake]] struck Tokyo in [[1923]], killing approximately 70,000 people; a massive reconstruction plan was drawn up, but was too expensive to carry out except in part. Despite this, the city grew until the beginning of [[World War II]]. During the war, Tokyo was bombed heavily, and its population in [[1945]] was only half that of [[1940]].

After the war, Tokyo recovered and continued to grow, though the current [[recession]] affecting Japan has hurt the city.

Famous parts of the city and other sights include:
*[[Akihabara]]
*[[Asakusa]]
*[[Ginza]]
*[[Roppongi]]

[[fr:Tokyo]][[eo:Tokio]][[de:Tokio]] '''Tokyo''' (東京) is the capital and largest city of [[Japan]]. It is the capital of Tokyo [[prefecture]] and is located next to Tokyo Bay at the heart of the [[Kanto]] region.
[[Image:Japon_Tokyo1.jpg]]
The population of Tokyo is around 8 million, with a total of more than 12 million in the metropolitan area. Tokyo (along with it's suburbs) is considered to be the largest city in the world. (see [[Largest cities of the world]]). Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], the city was known as (江戸) "Edo." The [[Tokugawa]] [[shogunate]] was established in [[1603]] with Edo as its de facto capital. (Since the emperor's residence was in [[Kyoto]], that city was formally the capital of Japan.) In [[1868]], when the shogunate came to an end, the city was renamed "Tokyo" which means "Eastern Capital"; during the restoration, the emperor moved to Tokyo, making the city the formal as well as de facto capital of Japan. A major [[earthquake]] struck Tokyo in [[1923]], killing approximately 70,000 people; a massive reconstruction plan was drawn up, but was too expensive to carry out except in part. Despite this, the city grew until the beginning of [[World War II]]. During the war, Tokyo was [[Bombing of Tokyo in World War II|heavily bombed]], and its population in [[1945]] was only half that of [[1940]]. After the war, Tokyo recovered and continued to grow, though the current [[recession]] affecting Japan has hurt the city. Tokyo was host to the [[1964 Summer Olympics]]. In [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system in which 12 people were killed and thousands incapacitated. Famous parts of the city and other sights include: *[[Akihabara]] *[[Asakusa]] *[[Ginza]] *[[Harajuku]] *[[Roppongi]] *[[Shinjuku]] *[[Shibuya]]

On [[March 20]] [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin]] [[nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system in which 12 people were killed and thousands incapacitated. [[fr:Tokyo]][[eo:Tokio]][[de:Tokio]][[en:Tokyo]][[fr:Tokyo]][[eo:Tokio]][[de:Tokio]][[en:Tokyo]][[ja:東京特別区]][[zh:东京]] '''Tokyo''' (東京, literally: "eastern capital") has three distinct geographical meanings. 1. [[Tokyo prefecture]] is the sole local government with the name of Tokyo. It has population of 12,064,101 and area of 2186.9 km2. 2. While there is no municipality called Tokyo, the old Tokyo city that existed until 1943, covering the area of the present 23 [[special wards]], is still regarded as the single largest city and the [[capital]] of [[Japan]] with population of 8,134,688 and area of 621.3 km2. 3. The [[metropolitan area]] of south [[Kanto region]] consisting of Tokyo and three neighboring [[Kanagawa]], [[Saitama]] and [[Chiba]] prefectures is often regarded as the greater Tokyo area, as shown in the name of [[New Tokyo International Airport]] and [[Tokyo Disney Resort]] both located in Chiba prefecture. The four prefectures as a whole have population of 33,418,366 and area of 13,555.8 km2. ''The population and area figures are according to the Population Census in 2000. ''
[[Image:Japon_Tokyo1.jpg]]
The following description is concerned with Tokyo as a city of the second meaning. Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], the city was known as '''[[Edo]]''' (江戸). The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[shogunate]] was established in [[1603]] with Edo as its seat of government (de facto capital). (The emperor's residence, and formal capital, remained in [[Kyoto]], that city had been the actual capital of Japan until that time.) In [[1868]], when the shogunate came to an end, the city was renamed "Tokyo" which means "Eastern Capital"; during the restoration, the emperor moved to Tokyo, making the city the formal as well as de facto capital of Japan. A major [[earthquake]] struck Tokyo in [[1923]], killing approximately 70,000 people; a massive reconstruction plan was drawn up, but was too expensive to carry out except in part. Despite this, the city grew until the beginning of [[World War II]]. During the war, Tokyo was [[Bombing of Tokyo in World War II|heavily bombed]], and its population in [[1945]] was only half that of [[1940]]. After the war, Tokyo recovered and continued to grow, and was host to the [[1964 Summer Olympics]]. Owing to the rapid economic growth of Japan until 1980’s, Tokyo became one of the world's most vital major cities. Although the [[recession]] after the burst of bubbles in early 1990’s has hurt the city, Tokyo is now going to be revitalized with many urban development projects and people’s return to the downtown Tokyo. On [[March 20]] [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin]] [[nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system in which 12 people were killed and thousands incapacitated. Famous parts of the city and other sights include: *[[Akihabara]] *[[Asakusa]] *[[Ginza]] *[[Harajuku]] *[[Roppongi]] *[[Shinjuku]] *[[Shibuya]] *[[Kokyo|Japanese Imperial Palace]] (''Kokyo'') *[[Tourism in Japan|Japanese landmarks]] *[[Tokyo Tower]] Airports: *[[New Tokyo International Airport]] (Narita Airport), in [[Narita, Japan]] *[[Tokyo International Airport]] (Haneda Airport), in [[Tokyo, Japan]]

[[fr:Tokyo]][[de:Tokio]][[en:Tokyo]][[eo:Tokio]][[ja:東京特別区]][[zh:东京]]
[[Image:Japon_Tokyo1.jpg]]
Tokyo is the capital city of Japan (see definitions of the prefecture, metropolitan area and city below). Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], the city was known as '''[[Edo]]''' (江戸). The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[shogunate]] was established in [[1603]] with Edo as its seat of government (de facto capital). (The emperor's residence, and formal capital, remained in [[Kyoto]], that city had been the actual capital of Japan until that time.) In [[1868]], when the shogunate came to an end, the city was renamed "Tokyo" which means "Eastern Capital"; during the restoration, the emperor moved to Tokyo, making the city the formal as well as de facto capital of Japan. A major [[earthquake]] struck Tokyo in [[1923]], killing approximately 70,000 people; a massive reconstruction plan was drawn up, but was too expensive to carry out except in part. Despite this, the city grew until the beginning of [[World War II]]. During the war, Tokyo was [[Bombing of Tokyo in World War II|heavily bombed]], and its population in [[1945]] was only half that of [[1940]]. General MacArthur established his Occupation headquarters in what is now the Dai-Ichi Seimei building overlooking the Imperial Palace and, in the post-war years, and especially stimulated by the Korean War, Japan experienced an economic miracle that led it from post-war deprivation to tremendous economic success. In the process, Japan entered and very often came to dominate a range of industries including steel, shipbuilding, automobiles, semi-conductors, consumer electronics. Although the [[recession]] following the bursting of the "bubble economy" in the early 1990’s hurt the city, Tokyo has become one of the most dynamic capital cities on earth. It has a trememdous range of social and economic activities, with myriad restaurants and clubs, a major financial district, tremendous industrial strength, a wealth of shops and entertainment opportunities. The investment boom of the late 1980's is perhaps the greatest the world has ever known (as judged e.g. by the level of building expenditures in relation to the size of the economy) and, as a result, Tokyo has an enormously more modern capital stock (of buildings) than e.g. London or New York. On [[March 20]] [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin]] [[nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system (in the tunnels beneath the political district of central Tokyo) in which 12 people were killed and thousands affected. Tokyo is home to the world's most complex transit/train system and is world-famous for its crowded rush hour. '''Tokyo''' (東京, literally: "eastern capital") has three distinct geographical meanings. 1. [[Tokyo prefecture]] is the sole local government with the name of Tokyo. It has population of 12,064,101 and area of 2186.9 km2. 2. While there is no municipality called Tokyo, the old Tokyo city that existed until 1943, covering the area of the present 23 [[special wards]], is still regarded as the single largest city and the [[capital]] of [[Japan]] with population of 8,134,688 and area of 621.3 km2. 3. The [[metropolitan area]] of south [[Kanto region]] consisting of Tokyo and three neighboring [[Kanagawa]], [[Saitama]] and [[Chiba]] prefectures is often regarded as the greater Tokyo area, as shown in the name of [[New Tokyo International Airport]] and [[Tokyo Disney Resort]] both located in Chiba prefecture. The four prefectures as a whole have population of 33,418,366 and area of 13,555.8 km2. ''The population and area figures are according to the Population Census in 2000. '' Some famous places for sight-seeing include: *[[Kokyo|Japanese Imperial Palace]] (''Kokyo'') *[[Tourism in Japan|Japanese landmarks]] *[[Tokyo Tower]] Airports: *[[New Tokyo International Airport]] (Narita Airport), in [[Narita, Japan]] *[[Tokyo International Airport]] (Haneda Airport), in [[Tokyo, Japan]]

'''Tokyo''' is the capital city of [[Japan]] (see definitions of the prefecture, metropolitan area and city below). ===History=== Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], the city was known as '''[[Edo]]''' (江戸). The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[shogunate]] was established in [[1603]] with Edo as its seat of government (de facto capital). (The emperor's residence, and formal capital, remained in [[Kyoto]], that city had been the actual capital of Japan until that time.) In [[1868]], when the shogunate came to an end, the city was renamed "Tokyo" which means "Eastern Capital"; during the restoration, the emperor moved to Tokyo, making the city the formal as well as de facto capital of Japan. A major [[earthquake]] struck Tokyo in [[1923]], killing approximately 70,000 people; a massive reconstruction plan was drawn up, but was too expensive to carry out except in part. Despite this, the city grew until the beginning of [[World War II]]. During the war, Tokyo was [[Bombing of Tokyo in World War II|heavily bombed]], much of the city was burned to the ground, and its population in [[1945]] was only half that of [[1940]]. [[Douglas MacArthur|General Douglas MacArthur]] established his Occupation headquarters in what is now the Dai-Ichi Seimei building overlooking the Imperial Palace and, in the post-war years, and especially stimulated by the [[Korean War]], Japan experienced an economic miracle that led it from post-war deprivation to tremendous economic success. In the process, Japan entered and very often came to dominate a range of industries including steel, shipbuilding, automobiles, semi-conductors, consumer electronics. Although the [[recession]] following the bursting of the "bubble economy" in the early [[1990s]] hurt the city, Tokyo has become one of the most dynamic capital cities on earth. It has a tremendous range of social and economic activities, with myriad restaurants and clubs, a major financial district, tremendous industrial strength, a wealth of shops and entertainment opportunities. The investment boom of the late [[1980s]] is perhaps the greatest the world has ever known (as judged e.g. by the level of building expenditures in relation to the size of the economy) and, as a result, Tokyo has an enormously more modern capital stock (of buildings) than, e.g., London or New York. On [[March 20]] [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin]] [[nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system (in the tunnels beneath the political district of central Tokyo) in which 12 people were killed and thousands affected. ===Today=== The city today is one of the world's most important urban centres. It is a major business and financial centre as well as the political capital of Japan. The city is unusual in that it has far fewer skyscrapers than other cities of its size, mostly due to earthqaukes. Rather it is mostly comprised of eiter lowrise appartments of six to ten floors or of densly packed family homes. Tokyo is also home to the world's most complex transit/train system and is world-famous for its crowded rush hour. It has population of 12,064,101 and area of 2186.9 km2. 2. While there is no municipality called Tokyo, the old Tokyo city that existed until 1943, covering the area of the present 23 [[special wards]], is still regarded as the single largest city and the [[capital]] of [[Japan]] with population of 8,134,688 and area of 621.3 km2. 3. The [[metropolitan area]] of south [[Kanto region]] consisting of Tokyo and three neighboring [[Kanagawa]], [[Saitama]] and [[Chiba]] prefectures is often regarded as the greater Tokyo area, as shown in the name of [[New Tokyo International Airport]] and [[Tokyo Disney]] Resort both located in Chiba prefecture. The four prefectures as a whole have population of 33,418,366 and area of 13,555.8 km2. ''The population and area figures are according to the Population Census in 2000. '' Some famous places for sight-seeing include: *[[Kokyo|Japanese Imperial Palace]] (''Kokyo'') *[[Tourism in Japan|Japanese landmarks]] *[[Tokyo Tower]] *[[Tokyo National Museum]] *[[Edo-Tokyo Museum]] *[[Ginza]] shopping district Airports: *[[New Tokyo International Airport]] (Narita Airport), in [[Narita, Japan]] *[[Tokyo International Airport]] (Haneda Airport), in [[Tokyo, Japan]]

[[de:Tokio]] [[eo:Tokio]] [[es:Tokio]] [[fr:Tokyo]] [[ja:東京都]] [[pl:Tōkyō]] [[pt:Tóquio]] [[zh:东京]] '''Tokyo''' (東京, literally: "eastern capital") is the capital and largest city of [[Japan]]. A little more than 12 million people live in the city while hundreds of thousands of others commute everyday from surrounding areas to work and do business in the city. Tokyo is the business center of the country as well as being the home of the [[Japanese emperor]] and the seat of the national government. The city is well known for its highly modern skyscrapers, thousands of flashing neon signs, a bustling network of roads always filled with traffic, and a very extensive [[metro|underground railway system]]. Tokyo occupies the ''Tokyo prefecture'' (東京都; Tokyo-to, Tokyo prefecture), which is located in the [[Kanto region]] on [[Honshu]] [[island]]. The prefecture is sometimes referred to as the ''Tokyo Metropolitan Prefecture'' or ''Tokyo Metropolitan Area''. This stems from the fact that the character used for ''prefecture'' for Tokyo differs from the character used for other prefectures. Tokyo is often considered part of the [[Greater Tokyo Area]], which consists of Tokyo prefecture itself and the surrounding prefectures of [[Kanagawa]], [[Saitama]] and [[Chiba]]. The Greater Tokyo area is the largest metropolitan area in the world having a population of 33,418,366. The government's name of prefecture and city is '''Tokyo Metropolitan Government'''. The offices are located in the ward of [[Shinjuku]]. While there is no municipality called Tokyo, the old Tokyo city that existed until [[1943]], covering the area of the present [[23 special wards]], is still regarded as the single largest city and the [[capital]] of [[Japan]] with population of 8,134,688 and area of 621.3 km2. This designation was made in [[1943]] in order to unify Tokyo City (東京市; Tokyo-shi) and the existing Tokyo Prefecture (東京府; Tokyo-fu) into the nation's capital. Currently, there are no defined city limits of Tokyo-shi, so Tokyo-shi and Tokyo-to are relatively synonomous. According to the Population Census in 2000, Tokyo has population of 12,064,101 and area of 2186.9 km2.

== History == Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], the city was known as '''[[Edo]]''' (江戸). The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[shogunate]] was established in [[1603]] with Edo as its seat of government (de facto capital). (The emperor's residence, and formal capital, remained in [[Kyoto]], that city had been the actual capital of Japan until that time.) In [[1868]], when the shogunate came to an end, the city was renamed "Tokyo" which means "Eastern Capital"; during the restoration, the emperor moved to Tokyo, making the city the formal as well as de facto capital of Japan. A major [[earthquake]] struck Tokyo in [[1923]], killing approximately 70,000 people; a massive reconstruction plan was drawn up, but was too expensive to carry out except in part. Despite this, the city grew until the beginning of [[World War II]]. During the war, Tokyo was [[Bombing of Tokyo in World War II|heavily bombed]], much of the city was burned to the ground, and its population in [[1945]] was only half that of [[1940]]. [[Douglas MacArthur|General Douglas MacArthur]] established his Occupation headquarters in what is now the Dai-Ichi Seimei building overlooking the Imperial Palace and, in the post-war years, and especially stimulated by the [[Korean War]], Japan experienced an economic miracle that led it from post-war deprivation to tremendous economic success. In the process, Japan entered and very often came to dominate a range of industries including steel, shipbuilding, automobiles, semi-conductors, consumer electronics. Although the [[recession]] following the bursting of the "bubble economy" in the early [[1990s]] hurt the city, Tokyo has become one of the most dynamic capital cities on earth. It has a tremendous range of social and economic activities, with myriad restaurants and clubs, a major financial district, tremendous industrial strength, a wealth of shops and entertainment opportunities. The investment boom of the late [[1980s]] is perhaps the greatest the world has ever known (as judged e.g. by the level of building expenditures in relation to the size of the economy) and, as a result, Tokyo has an enormously more modern capital stock (of buildings) than, e.g., London or New York. On [[March 20]] [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin]] [[nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system (in the tunnels beneath the political district of central Tokyo) in which 12 people were killed and thousands affected. ===Today=== The city today is one of the world's most important urban centres. It is a major business and financial centre as well as the political capital of Japan. The city is unusual in that it has far fewer skyscrapers than other cities of its size, mostly due to earthquake construction codes. Rather it is mostly comprised of either low-rise apartments of six to ten floors or of densely packed family homes. Tokyo is also home to the world's most complex transit/train system and is world-famous for its crowded rush hours.
[[Image:Japon_Tokyo1.jpg]]
Tokyo by night
== Geography == Tokyo prefecture is divided into mainland and island areas. The mainland is located at the northwest of [[Tokyo Bay]], about 90 km east to west and 25 km north to south. It borders [[Chiba prefecture]] in the east, [[Yamanashi prefecture]] in the west, [[Kanagawa prefecture]] in the south, and [[Saitama prefecture]] in the north. The islands are made up of [[Izu Islands]] and [[Ogasawara Islands]], stretching 1,000km in the [[Pacific Ocean]]. ===Wards=== Tokyo prefecture has [[23 special wards]] in an area of about 621 square kilometers. As of September 1, 2002 the total population of the 23 wards was about 8.28 million, with a population density of 13,333 persons per square kilometer. The prefecture is sometimes referred to as the ''Tokyo Metropolitan Prefecture'' or ''Tokyo Metropolitan Area''. This stems from the fact that the character used for ''prefecture'' for Tokyo, which can also be read as ''capital'' or ''metropolis'', differs from the character used for other prefectures. Tokyo is often considered part of the [[Greater Tokyo Area]], which consists of Tokyo prefecture itself and the surrounding prefectures of [[Kanagawa]], [[Saitama]] and [[Chiba]]. The Greater Tokyo area is the largest metropolitan area in the world having a population of 33,418,366. The government's name of prefecture and city is '''Tokyo Metropolitan Government'''. The offices are located in the ward of [[Shinjuku]]. While there is no municipality called Tokyo, the old Tokyo city that existed until [[1943]], covering the area of the present [[23 special wards]], is still regarded as the single largest city and the [[capital]] of [[Japan]] with population of 8,134,688 and area of 621.3 km2. This designation was made in [[1943]] in order to unify Tokyo City (東京市; Tōkyō-shi) and the existing Tokyo Prefecture (東京府; Tōkyō-fu) into the nation's capital. Currently, there are no defined city limits of Tokyo-shi, so Tokyo-shi and Tokyo-to are relatively synonymous. ==History==
[[Image:Karte_Tokia_MKL1888.png|German Map of Tokyo, Japan from 1888]]
1888 German Map of Tokyo, Japan
Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], the city was known as '''[[Edo]]''' (江戸). The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[shogunate]] was established in [[1603]] with Edo as its seat of government (de facto capital). (The emperor's residence, and formal capital, remained in [[Kyoto]], that city had been the actual capital of Japan until that time.) In [[1868]], when the shogunate came to an end, the city was renamed "Tokyo" which means "Eastern Capital"; during the restoration, the emperor moved to Tokyo, making the city the formal as well as de facto capital of Japan. A major [[earthquake]] struck Tokyo in [[1923]], killing approximately 70,000 people; a massive reconstruction plan was drawn up, but was too expensive to carry out except in part. Despite this, the city grew until the beginning of [[World War II]]. During the war, Tokyo was [[Bombing of Tokyo in World War II|heavily bombed]], much of the city was burned to the ground, and its population in [[1945]] was only half that of [[1940]]. [[Douglas MacArthur|General Douglas MacArthur]] established his Occupation headquarters in what is now the Dai-Ichi Seimei building overlooking the Imperial Palace and, in the post-war years, and especially stimulated by the [[Korean War]], Japan experienced an economic miracle that led it from post-war deprivation to tremendous economic success. In the process, Japan entered and very often came to dominate a range of industries including steel, shipbuilding, automobiles, semi-conductors, consumer electronics. Although the [[recession]] following the bursting of the "bubble economy" in the early [[1990s]] hurt the city, Tokyo has become one of the most dynamic capital cities on earth. It has a tremendous range of social and economic activities, with myriad restaurants and clubs, a major financial district, tremendous industrial strength, a wealth of shops and entertainment opportunities. The investment boom of the late [[1980s]] is perhaps the greatest the world has ever known (as judged e.g. by the level of building expenditures in relation to the size of the economy) and, as a result, Tokyo has an enormously more modern capital stock (of buildings) than, e.g., London or New York. On [[March 20]] [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin]] [[nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system (in the tunnels beneath the political district of central Tokyo) in which 12 people were killed and thousands affected. ==Tokyo Today== The city today is one of the world's most important urban centres. It is a major business and financial centre as well as the political capital of Japan. The city is unusual in that it has far fewer skyscrapers than other cities of its size, mostly due to earthquake construction codes. Rather it is mostly comprised of either low-rise apartments of six to ten floors or of densely packed family homes. Tokyo is also home to the world's most complex transit/train system and is world-famous for its crowded rush hours.
[[Image:Japon_Tokyo1.jpg]]
Tokyo by night
== Geography == Tokyo prefecture is divided into mainland and island areas. The mainland is located at the northwest of [[Tokyo Bay]], about 90 km east to west and 25 km north to south. It borders [[Chiba prefecture]] in the east, [[Yamanashi prefecture]] in the west, [[Kanagawa prefecture]] in the south, and [[Saitama prefecture]] in the north. The islands are made up of [[Izu Islands]] and [[Ogasawara Islands]], stretching 1,000km in the [[Pacific Ocean]]. ===Wards=== Tokyo prefecture has [[23 special wards]] in an area of about 621 square kilometers. As of September 1, 2002 the total population of the 23 wards was about 8.28 million, with a population density of 13,333 persons per square kilometer. Each ward is a local [[municipality]] with its own elected mayors and assemblies: *[[Adachi]] *[[Arakawa]] *[[Bunkyo]] *[[Chiyoda]] *[[Chuo]] *[[Edogawa]] *[[Itabashi]] *[[Katsushika]] *[[Kita]] *[[Koto]] *[[Meguro]] *[[Minato]] *[[Nakano]] *[[Nerima]] *[[Ota]] *[[Setagaya]] *[[Shibuya]] *[[Shinagawa]] *[[Shinjuku]] *[[Suginami]] *[[Sumida]] *[[Toshima]] *[[Taito]] ===List of Cities=== In addition to wards, the prefecture has cities like other prefectures. *[[Akiruno]] *[[Akishima]] *[[Chofu]] *[[Fuchu, Tokyo|Fuchu]] *[[Fussa]] *[[Hachioji]] *[[Hamura]] *[[Higashikurume]] *[[Higashimurayama]] *[[Higashiyamato]] *[[Hino]] *[[Inagi]] *[[Kiyose]] *[[Kodaira]] *[[Koganei]] *[[Kokubunji]] *[[Komae]] *[[Kunitachi]] *[[Machida]] *[[Mitaka]] *[[Musashimurayama]] *[[Musashino]] *[[Nishitokyo]] *[[Ome]] *[[Tachikawa]] *[[Tama]] === Districts, Sub-prefecture, towns and villages === *[[Nishitama, Tokyo|Nishitama]] **[[Hibara, Tokyo|Hibara]] **[[Hinode, Tokyo|Hinode]] **[[Mizuho, Tokyo|Mizuho]] **[[Okutama, Tokyo|Okutama]] The following are towns and villages on islands. *[[Hachijo sub-prefecture]] **[[Aogashima, Tokyo|Aogashima]] **[[Hachijo, Tokyo|Hachijo]] *[[Miyake sub-prefecture]] **[[Mikurajima, Tokyo|Mikurajima]] **[[Miyake, Tokyo|Miyake]] *[[Ogasawara sub-prefecture]] **[[Ogasawara, Tokyo|Ogasawara]] *[[Oshima sub-prefecture]] **[[Kozushima, Tokyo|Kozushima]] **[[Niijima, Tokyo|Niijima]] **[[Oshima, Tokyo|Oshima]] **[[Toshima, Tokyo|Toshima]] ''The list is in their standard codes for areas of prefectures and municipalities for statistical use.'' == Economy == Tokyo is the economic center of Japan. Many prominent companies are headquartered in Tokyo. [[CASIO]] and [[SEGA]] (a division [[Sega of Japan]]) are headquartered in Tokyo. == Demographics == == Culture == == Tourism == Some famous places for sight-seeing include: *[[Kokyo|Japanese Imperial Palace]] (''Kokyo'') *[[Tourism in Japan|Japanese landmarks]] *[[Tokyo Tower]] *[[Tokyo National Museum]] *[[Edo-Tokyo Museum]] *[[Ginza]] shopping district *[[Roppongi Hills]] One of Tokyo's largest skyscrapers == Prefectural symbols == == Miscellaneous topics == === Transportation === Airports: *[[New Tokyo International Airport]] (Narita Airport), [[Narita]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] *[[Tokyo International Airport]] (Haneda Airport), [[Ota]], Tokyo ==External links== * [http://www.chijihonbu.metro.tokyo.jp/english/ Official Tokyo prefecture homepage] ==Sources== Alternate spelling from [[1911 Encyclopaedia Britannica]] article

[[de:Tokio]] [[eo:Tokio]] [[es:Tokio]] [[fr:Tokyo]] [[ja:東京都]] [[nl:Tokio]] [[pl:Tōkyō]] [[pt:Tóquio]] [[zh:东京]]
[[Image:tokyotower.jpg|tokyotower.jpg]]
''[[Tokyo Tower]] and [[Shiba Park]]''
'''Tōkyō''' (東京; lit. eastern capital) is the capital and largest city of [[Japan]]. A little more than 12 million people live in the city while hundreds of thousands of others commute everyday from surrounding areas to work and do business in the city. Tokyo is the business center of the country as well as the home of the [[Japanese emperor]] and the seat of the national government. The city is well known for its highly modern skyscrapers, thousands of flashing neon signs, a bustling network of roads always filled with traffic, and a very extensive [[metro|underground railway system]]. Tokyo occupies the '''Tokyo prefecture''' (東京都; Tōkyō-to, Tokyo prefecture), which is located in the [[Kanto region]] on [[Honshu]] [[island]]. The prefecture is sometimes referred to as the ''Tokyo Metropolitan Prefecture'' or ''Tokyo Metropolitan Area''. This stems from the fact that the character used for ''prefecture'' for Tokyo, which can also be read as ''capital'' or ''metropolis'', differs from the character used for other prefectures. Tokyo is often considered part of the [[Greater Tokyo Area]], which consists of Tokyo prefecture itself and the surrounding prefectures of [[Kanagawa]], [[Saitama]] and [[Chiba]]. The Greater Tokyo area is the largest metropolitan area in the world with a population of 33,418,366. The government's name of prefecture and city is '''Tokyo Metropolitan Government'''. The offices are located in the ward of [[Shinjuku]]. While there is no municipality called Tokyo, the old Tokyo city that existed until [[1943]], covering the area of the present [[23 special wards]], is still regarded as the single largest city and the [[capital]] of [[Japan]] with population of 8,134,688 and area of 621.3 km2. This designation was made in [[1943]] in order to unify Tokyo City (東京市; Tōkyō-shi) and the existing Tokyo Prefecture (東京府; Tōkyō-fu) into the nation's capital. Currently, there are no defined city limits of Tokyo-shi, so Tokyo-shi and Tokyo-to are relatively synonymous. According to the Population Census in 2000, Tokyo has population of 12,064,101 and area of 2186.9 km2. Tokyo literally means "eastern capital" in [[Japanese language|Japanese]], to mean to oppose to an old capital in west, [[Kyoto]]. It is previously alternately spelled '''Tokio''' in [[English language|English]], and still spelled Tokio in some other languages like [[Dutch language|Dutch]], [[Esperanto]], [[German language|German]], and [[Spanish language|Spanish]].
'''Tokyo prefecture (東京都)'''

==History==
[[Image:Karte_Tokia_MKL1888.png|German Map of Tokyo, Japan from 1888]]
1888 German Map of Tokyo, Japan
Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], the city was known as '''[[Edo]]''' (江戸). The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[shogunate]] was established in [[1603]] with Edo as its seat of government (de facto capital). (The emperor's residence, and formal capital, remained in [[Kyoto]], that city had been the actual capital of Japan until that time.) In [[1868]], when the shogunate came to an end, the city was renamed "Tokyo" which means "Eastern Capital"; during the restoration, the emperor moved to Tokyo, making the city the formal as well as de facto capital of Japan. A major [[earthquake]] struck Tokyo in [[1923]], killing approximately 70,000 people; a massive reconstruction plan was drawn up, but was too expensive to carry out except in part. Despite this, the city grew until the beginning of [[World War II]]. During the war, Tokyo was [[Bombing of Tokyo in World War II|heavily bombed]], much of the city was burned to the ground, and its population in [[1945]] was only half that of [[1940]]. [[Douglas MacArthur|General Douglas MacArthur]] established his Occupation headquarters in what is now the Dai-Ichi Seimei building overlooking the Imperial Palace and, in the post-war years, and especially stimulated by the [[Korean War]], Japan experienced an economic miracle that led it from post-war deprivation to tremendous economic success. In the process, Japan entered and very often came to dominate a range of industries including steel, shipbuilding, automobiles, semi-conductors, consumer electronics. Although the [[recession]] following the bursting of the "bubble economy" in the early [[1990s]] hurt the city, Tokyo has become one of the most dynamic capital cities on earth. It has a tremendous range of social and economic activities, with myriad restaurants and clubs, a major financial district, tremendous industrial strength, a wealth of shops and entertainment opportunities. The investment boom of the late [[1980s]] is perhaps the greatest the world has ever known (as judged e.g. by the level of building expenditures in relation to the size of the economy) and, as a result, Tokyo has an enormously more modern capital stock (of buildings) than, e.g., London or New York. On [[March 20]] [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin]] [[nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system (in the tunnels beneath the political district of central Tokyo) in which 12 people were killed and thousands affected. ==Tokyo Today== The city today is one of the world's most important urban centres. It is a major business and financial centre as well as the political capital of Japan. The city is unusual in that it has far fewer skyscrapers than other cities of its size, mostly due to earthquake construction codes. Rather it is mostly comprised of either low-rise apartments of six to ten floors or of densely packed family homes. Tokyo is also home to the world's most complex transit/train system and is world-famous for its crowded rush hours.

== Geography == Tokyo prefecture is divided into mainland and island areas. The mainland is located at the northwest of [[Tokyo Bay]], about 90 km east to west and 25 km north to south. It borders [[Chiba prefecture]] in the east, [[Yamanashi prefecture]] in the west, [[Kanagawa prefecture]] in the south, and [[Saitama prefecture]] in the north. The islands are made up of [[Izu Islands]] and [[Ogasawara Islands]], stretching 1,000km in the [[Pacific Ocean]]. ===Wards=== Tokyo prefecture has [[23 special wards]] in an area of about 621 square kilometers. [[de:Tokio]] [[eo:Tokio]] [[es:Tokio]] [[fr:Tokyo]] [[ja:東京都]] [[nl:Tokio]] [[pl:Tōkyō]] [[pt:Tóquio]] [[zh:东京]] '''Tōkyō''' (東京; lit. eastern capital) is the capital and largest city of [[Japan]].
[[Image:tokyotower.jpg|tokyotower.jpg]]
''[[Tokyo Tower]] and [[Shiba Park]]''
A little more than 12 million people live in the city while hundreds of thousands of others commute everyday from surrounding areas to work and do business in the city. Tokyo is the business center of the country as well as the home of the [[Japanese emperor]] and the seat of the national government. The city is well known for its highly modern skyscrapers, thousands of flashing neon signs, a bustling network of roads always filled with traffic, and a very extensive [[metro|underground railway system]]. Tokyo occupies the '''Tokyo prefecture''' (東京都; Tōkyō-to, Tokyo prefecture), which is located in the [[Kanto region]] on [[Honshu]] [[island]]. The prefecture is sometimes referred to as the ''Tokyo Metropolitan Prefecture'' or ''Tokyo Metropolitan Area''. This stems from the fact that the character used for ''prefecture'' for Tokyo, which can also be read as ''capital'' or ''metropolis'', differs from the character used for other prefectures. Tokyo is often considered part of the [[Greater Tokyo Area]], which consists of Tokyo prefecture itself and the surrounding prefectures of [[Kanagawa]], [[Saitama]] and [[Chiba]]. The Greater Tokyo area is the largest metropolitan area in the world with a population of 33,418,366. The government's name of prefecture and city is '''Tokyo Metropolitan Government'''. The offices are located in the ward of [[Shinjuku]]. While there is no municipality called Tokyo, the old Tokyo city that existed until [[1943]], covering the area of the present [[23 special wards]], is still regarded as the single largest city and the [[capital]] of [[Japan]] with population of 8,134,688 and area of 621.3 km2. This designation was made in [[1943]] in order to unify Tokyo City (東京市; Tōkyō-shi) and the existing Tokyo Prefecture (東京府; Tōkyō-fu) into the nation's capital. Currently, there are no defined city limits of Tokyo-shi, so Tokyo-shi and Tokyo-to are relatively synonymous. According to the Population Census in 2000, Tokyo has population of 12,064,101 and area of 2186.9 km2. Tokyo literally means "eastern capital" in [[Japanese language|Japanese]], to mean to oppose to an old capital in west, [[Kyoto]]. It is previously alternately spelled '''Tokio''' in [[English language|English]], and still spelled Tokio in some other languages like [[Dutch language|Dutch]], [[Esperanto]], [[German language|German]], and [[Spanish language|Spanish]].
'''Tokyo prefecture (東京都)'''

==History==
[[Image:Karte_Tokia_MKL1888.png|German Map of Tokyo, Japan from 1888]]
1888 [[Germany|German]] [[Map]] of Tokyo, Japan
Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], the city was known as '''[[Edo]]''' (江戸). The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[shogunate]] was established in [[1603]] with Edo as its seat of government (de facto capital). (The emperor's residence, and formal capital, remained in [[Kyoto]], that city had been the actual capital of Japan until that time.) In [[1868]], when the shogunate came to an end, the city was renamed "Tokyo" which means "Eastern Capital"; during the restoration, the emperor moved to Tokyo, making the city the formal as well as de facto capital of Japan. A major [[earthquake]] struck Tokyo in [[1923]], killing approximately 70,000 people; a massive reconstruction plan was drawn up, but was too expensive to carry out except in part. Despite this, the city grew until the beginning of [[World War II]]. During the war, Tokyo was [[Bombing of Tokyo in World War II|heavily bombed]], much of the city was burned to the ground, and its population in [[1945]] was only half that of [[1940]]. [[Douglas MacArthur|General Douglas MacArthur]] established his Occupation headquarters in what is now the Dai-Ichi Seimei building overlooking the Imperial Palace and, in the post-war years, and especially stimulated by the [[Korean War]], Japan experienced an economic miracle that led it from post-war deprivation to tremendous economic success. In the process, Japan entered and very often came to dominate a range of industries including steel, shipbuilding, automobiles, semi-conductors, consumer electronics. Although the [[recession]] following the bursting of the "bubble economy" in the early [[1990s]] hurt the city, Tokyo has become one of the most dynamic capital cities on earth. It has a tremendous range of social and economic activities, with myriad restaurants and clubs, a major financial district, tremendous industrial strength, a wealth of shops and entertainment opportunities. The investment boom of the late [[1980s]] is perhaps the greatest the world has ever known (as judged e.g. by the level of building expenditures in relation to the size of the economy) and, as a result, Tokyo has an enormously more modern capital stock (of buildings) than, e.g., London or New York. On [[March 20]] [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin]] [[nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system (in the tunnels beneath the political district of central Tokyo) in which 12 people were killed and thousands affected. ==Tokyo Today== The city today is one of the world's most important urban centres. It is a major business and financial centre as well as the political capital of Japan. The city is unusual in that it has far fewer skyscrapers than other cities of its size, mostly due to earthquake construction codes. Rather it is mostly comprised of either low-rise apartments of six to ten floors or of densely packed family homes. Tokyo is also home to the world's most complex transit/train system and is world-famous for its crowded rush hours. == Geography == Tokyo prefecture is divided into mainland and island areas. The mainland is located at the northwest of [[Tokyo Bay]], about 90 km east to west and 25 km north to south. It borders [[Chiba prefecture]] in the east, [[Yamanashi prefecture]] in the west, [[Kanagawa prefecture]] in the south, and [[Saitama prefecture]] in the north. The islands are made up of [[Izu Islands]] and [[Ogasawara Islands]], stretching 1,000km in the [[Pacific Ocean]]. ===Wards=== Tokyo prefecture has [[23 special wards]] in an area of about 621 square kilometers. As of September 1, 2002 the total population of the 23 wards was about 8.28 million, with a population density of 13,333 persons per square kilometer. Each ward is a local [[municipality]] with its own elected mayors and assemblies:

The Greater Tokyo area is the largest metropolitan area in the world with a population of 33,418,366. The government's name of prefecture and city is '''Tokyo Metropolitan Government'''. The offices are located in the ward of [[Shinjuku]]. While there is no municipality called Tokyo, the old Tokyo city that existed until [[1943]], covering the area of the present [[23 special wards]], is still regarded as the single largest city and the [[capital]] of [[Japan]] with population of 8,134,688 and area of 621.3 km2. This designation was made in [[1943]] in order to unify Tokyo City (東京市; Tōkyō-shi) and the existing Tokyo Prefecture (東京府; Tōkyō-fu) into the nation's capital. Currently, there are no defined city limits of Tokyo-shi, so Tokyo-shi and Tokyo-to are relatively synonymous. According to the Population Census in 2000, Tokyo has population of 12,064,101 and area of 2186.9 km2. Tokyo literally means "eastern capital" in [[Japanese language|Japanese]], to mean to oppose to an old capital in west, [[Kyoto]], which was renamed "Saikyo", meaning "western capital", for a period of time. It is previously alternately spelled '''Tokio''' in [[English language|English]], and still spelled Tokio in some other languages like [[Dutch language|Dutch]], [[Esperanto]], [[German language|German]], and [[Spanish language|Spanish]].

==History== Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], the city was known as '''[[Edo]]''' (江戸). The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[shogunate]] was established in [[1603]] with Edo as its seat of government (de facto capital). (The emperor's residence, and formal capital, remained in [[Kyoto]], that city had been the actual capital of Japan until that time.) In [[1868]], when the shogunate came to an end, the city was renamed "Tokyo" which means "Eastern Capital"; during the restoration, the emperor moved to Tokyo, making the city the formal as well as de facto capital of Japan. A major [[earthquake]] struck Tokyo in [[1923]], killing approximately 70,000 people; a massive reconstruction plan was drawn up, but was too expensive to carry out except in part. Despite this, the city grew until the beginning of [[World War II]]. During the war, Tokyo was [[Bombing of Tokyo in World War II|heavily bombed]], much of the city was burned to the ground, and its population in [[1945]] was only half that of [[1940]]. [[Douglas MacArthur|General Douglas MacArthur]] established his Occupation headquarters in what is now the Dai-Ichi Seimei building overlooking the Imperial Palace and, in the post-war years, and especially stimulated by the [[Korean War]], Japan experienced an economic miracle that led it from post-war deprivation to tremendous economic success. In the process, Japan entered and very often came to dominate a range of industries including steel, shipbuilding, automobiles, semi-conductors, consumer electronics. Although the [[recession]] following the bursting of the "bubble economy" in the early [[1990s]] hurt the city, Tokyo has become one of the most dynamic capital cities on earth. It has a tremendous range of social and economic activities, with myriad restaurants and clubs, a major financial district, tremendous industrial strength, a wealth of shops and entertainment opportunities. The investment boom of the late [[1980s]] is perhaps the greatest the world has ever known (as judged e.g. by the level of building expenditures in relation to the size of the economy) and, as a result, Tokyo has an enormously more modern capital stock (of buildings) than, e.g., [[London]] or [[New York City]]. On [[March 20]] [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin]] [[nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system (in the tunnels beneath the political district of central Tokyo) in which 12 people were killed and thousands affected.
[[Image:Karte_Tokia_MKL1888.png|German Map of Tokyo, Japan from 1888]]
1888 [[Germany|German]] [[Map]] of Tokyo, Japan
==Tokyo Today== The city today is one of the world's most important urban centres. It is a major business and financial centre as well as the political capital of Japan. The city is unusual in that it has far fewer skyscrapers than other cities of its size, mostly due to earthquake construction codes. Rather it is mostly comprised of either low-rise apartments of six to ten floors or of densely packed family homes. Tokyo is also home to the world's most complex transit/train system and is world-famous for its crowded rush hours. == Geography == Tokyo prefecture is divided into mainland and island areas. The mainland is located at the northwest of [[Tokyo Bay]], about 90 km east to west and 25 km north to south. It borders [[Chiba prefecture]] in the east, [[Yamanashi prefecture]] in the west, [[Kanagawa prefecture]] in the south, and [[Saitama prefecture]] in the north. The islands are made up of [[Izu Islands]] and [[Ogasawara Islands]], stretching 1,000km in the [[Pacific Ocean]]. ===Wards=== Tokyo prefecture has [[23 special wards]] in an area of about 621 square kilometers. As of September 1, 2002 the total population of the 23 wards was about 8.28 million, with a population density of 13,333 persons per square kilometer. Each ward is a local [[municipality]] with its own elected mayors and assemblies:

[[Image:Japan_tokyo_map_small.png]]
According to the Population Census in 2000, Tokyo has population of 12,064,101 and area of 2186.9 km2. Tokyo literally means "eastern capital" in [[Japanese language|Japanese]], to mean to oppose to an old capital in west, [[Kyoto]], which was renamed "Saikyo", meaning "western capital", for a period of time. It is previously alternately spelled '''Tokio''' in [[English language|English]], and still spelled Tokio in some other languages like [[Dutch language|Dutch]], [[Esperanto]], [[German language|German]], and [[Spanish language|Spanish]]. ==History== Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], the city was known as '''[[Edo]]''' (江戸). The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[shogunate]] was established in [[1603]] with Edo as its seat of government (de facto capital). (The emperor's residence, and formal capital, remained in [[Kyoto]], that city had been the actual capital of Japan until that time.) In [[1868]], when the shogunate came to an end, the city was renamed "Tokyo" which means "Eastern Capital"; during the restoration, the emperor moved to Tokyo, making the city the formal as well as de facto capital of Japan. A major [[earthquake]] struck Tokyo in [[1923]], killing approximately 70,000 people; a massive reconstruction plan was drawn up, but was too expensive to carry out except in part. Despite this, the city grew until the beginning of [[World War II]]. During the war, Tokyo was [[Bombing of Tokyo in World War II|heavily bombed]], much of the city was burned to the ground, and its population in [[1945]] was only half that of [[1940]]. [[Douglas MacArthur|General Douglas MacArthur]] established his Occupation headquarters in what is now the Dai-Ichi Seimei building overlooking the Imperial Palace and, in the post-war years, and especially stimulated by the [[Korean War]], Japan experienced an economic miracle that led it from post-war deprivation to tremendous economic success. In the process, Japan entered and very often came to dominate a range of industries including steel, shipbuilding, automobiles, semi-conductors, consumer electronics.
[[Image:Karte_Tokia_MKL1888.png|German Map of Tokyo, Japan from 1888]]
1888 [[Germany|German]] [[Map]] of Tokyo, Japan
Although the [[recession]] following the bursting of the "bubble economy" in the early [[1990s]] hurt the city, Tokyo has become one of the most dynamic capital cities on earth. It has a tremendous range of social and economic activities, with myriad restaurants and clubs, a major financial district, tremendous industrial strength, a wealth of shops and entertainment opportunities. The investment boom of the late [[1980s]] is perhaps the greatest the world has ever known (as judged e.g. by the level of building expenditures in relation to the size of the economy) and, as a result, Tokyo has an enormously more modern capital stock (of buildings) than, e.g., [[London]] or [[New York City]]. On [[March 20]] [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin]] [[nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system (in the tunnels beneath the political district of central Tokyo) in which 12 people were killed and thousands affected. ==Tokyo today== The city today is one of the world's most important urban centres. It is a major business and financial centre as well as the political capital of Japan. The city is unusual in that it has far fewer skyscrapers than other cities of its size, mostly due to earthquake construction codes. Rather it is mostly comprised of either low-rise apartments of six to ten floors or of densely packed family homes. Tokyo is also home to the world's most complex transit/train system and is world-famous for its crowded rush hours. == Geography == Tokyo prefecture is divided into mainland and island areas. The mainland is located at the northwest of [[Tokyo Bay]], about 90 km east to west and 25 km north to south. It borders [[Chiba prefecture]] in the east, [[Yamanashi prefecture]] in the west, [[Kanagawa prefecture]] in the south, and [[Saitama prefecture]] in the north. The islands are made up of [[Izu Islands]] and [[Ogasawara Islands]], stretching 1,000km in the [[Pacific Ocean]].

===Wards=== Tokyo prefecture has [[23 special wards]] in an area of about 621 square kilometers. As of September 1, 2002 the total population of the 23 wards was about 8.28 million, with a population density of 13,333 persons per square kilometer. [[de:Tokio]] [[eo:Tokio]] [[es:Tokio]] [[fr:Tokyo]] [[ja:東京都]] [[nl:Tokio]] [[pl:Tōkyō]] [[pt:Tóquio]] [[zh:东京]]
[[Image:tokyotower.jpg|tokyotower.jpg]]
''[[Tokyo Tower]] and [[Shiba Park]]''
'''Tōkyō''' (東京; lit. eastern capital) is the capital and largest city of [[Japan]], and the most populous [[metropolitan area]] in the world. A little more than 12 million people live in the city while hundreds of thousands of others commute everyday from surrounding areas to work and do business in the city. Tokyo is the business center of the country as well as the home of the [[Japanese emperor]] and the seat of the national government. The city is well known for its highly modern skyscrapers, thousands of flashing neon signs, a bustling network of roads always filled with traffic, and a very extensive [[metro|underground railway system]]. Tokyo occupies the '''Tokyo prefecture''' (東京都; Tōkyō-to, Tokyo prefecture), which is located in the [[Kanto region]] on [[Honshu]] [[island]]. The prefecture is sometimes referred to as the ''Tokyo Metropolitan Prefecture'' or ''Tokyo Metropolitan Area''. This stems from the fact that the character used for ''prefecture'' for Tokyo, which can also be read as ''capital'' or ''metropolis'', differs from the character used for other prefectures. Tokyo is often considered part of the [[Greater Tokyo Area]], which consists of Tokyo prefecture itself and the surrounding prefectures of [[Kanagawa]], [[Saitama]] and [[Chiba]]. The Greater Tokyo area is the largest metropolitan area in the world with a population of 33,418,366. The government's name of prefecture and city is '''Tokyo Metropolitan Government'''. The offices are located in the ward of [[Shinjuku]]. While there is no municipality called Tokyo, the old Tokyo city that existed until [[1943]], covering the area of the present [[23 special wards]], is still regarded as the single largest city and the [[capital]] of [[Japan]] with population of 8,134,688 and area of 621.3 km2. This designation was made in [[1943]] in order to unify Tokyo City (東京市; Tōkyō-shi) and the existing Tokyo Prefecture (東京府; Tōkyō-fu) into the nation's capital. Currently, there are no defined city limits of Tokyo-shi, so Tokyo-shi and Tokyo-to are relatively synonymous.
'''Tokyo prefecture (東京都)'''

[[Douglas MacArthur|General Douglas MacArthur]] established his Occupation headquarters in what is now the Dai-Ichi Seimei building overlooking the Imperial Palace and, in the post-war years, and especially stimulated by the [[Korean War]], Japan experienced an economic miracle that led it from post-war deprivation to tremendous economic success. In the process, Japan entered and very often came to dominate a range of industries including steel, shipbuilding, automobiles, semi-conductors, consumer electronics. Although the [[recession]] following the bursting of the "bubble economy" in the early [[1990s]] hurt the city, Tokyo has become one of the most dynamic capital cities on earth. It has a tremendous range of social and economic activities, with myriad restaurants and clubs, a major financial district, tremendous industrial strength, a wealth of shops and entertainment opportunities. The investment boom of the late [[1980s]] is perhaps the greatest the world has ever known (as judged e.g. by the level of building expenditures in relation to the size of the economy) and, as a result, Tokyo has an enormously more modern capital stock (of buildings) than, e.g., [[London]] or [[New York City]].
[[Image:Karte_Tokia_MKL1888.png|German Map of Tokyo, Japan from 1888]]
1888 [[Germany|German]] [[Map]] of Tokyo, Japan
On [[March 20]] [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin]] [[nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system (in the tunnels beneath the political district of central Tokyo) in which 12 people were killed and thousands affected. ==Tokyo today== The city today is one of the world's most important urban centres. It is a major business and financial centre as well as the political capital of Japan. The city is unusual in that it has far fewer skyscrapers than other cities of its size, mostly due to earthquake construction codes. Rather it is mostly comprised of either low-rise apartments of six to ten floors or of densely packed family homes. Tokyo is also home to the world's most complex transit/train system and is world-famous for its crowded rush hours. == Geography == Tokyo prefecture is divided into mainland and island areas. The mainland is located at the northwest of [[Tokyo Bay]], about 90 km east to west and 25 km north to south. It borders [[Chiba prefecture]] in the east, [[Yamanashi prefecture]] in the west, [[Kanagawa prefecture]] in the south, and [[Saitama prefecture]] in the north. The islands are made up of [[Izu Islands]] and [[Ogasawara Islands]], stretching 1,000km in the [[Pacific Ocean]].

[[de:Tokio]] [[eo:Tokio]] [[es:Tokio]] [[fr:Tokyo]] [[ja:東京都]] [[nl:Tokio]] [[pl:Tōkyō]] [[pt:Tóquio]] [[zh:东京]]
[[Image:tokyotower.jpg|tokyotower.jpg]]
''[[Tokyo Tower]] and [[Shiba Park]]''
'''Tōkyō''' (東京; lit. eastern capital) is the capital and largest city of [[Japan]], and the most populous [[metropolitan area]] in the world. A little more than 12 million people live in the city while hundreds of thousands of others commute everyday from surrounding areas to work and do business in the city. Tokyo is the business center of the country as well as the home of the [[Japanese emperor]] and the seat of the national government. The city is well known for its highly modern skyscrapers, thousands of flashing neon signs, a bustling network of roads always filled with traffic, and a very extensive [[metro|underground railway system]]. Tokyo occupies the '''Tokyo prefecture''' (東京都; Tōkyō-to, Tokyo prefecture), which is located in the [[Kanto region]] on [[Honshu]] [[island]]. The prefecture is sometimes referred to as the ''Tokyo Metropolitan Prefecture'' or ''Tokyo Metropolitan Area''. This stems from the fact that the character used for ''prefecture'' for Tokyo, which can also be read as ''capital'' or ''metropolis'', differs from the character used for other prefectures. Tokyo is often considered part of the [[Greater Tokyo Area]], which consists of Tokyo prefecture itself and the surrounding prefectures of [[Kanagawa]], [[Saitama]] and [[Chiba]]. The Greater Tokyo area is the largest metropolitan area in the world with a population of 33,418,366. The government's name of prefecture and city is '''Tokyo Metropolitan Government'''. The offices are located in the ward of [[Shinjuku]]. While there is no municipality called Tokyo, the old Tokyo city that existed until [[1943]], covering the area of the present [[23 special wards]], is still regarded as the single largest city and the [[capital]] of [[Japan]] with population of 8,134,688 and area of 621.3 km2. This designation was made in [[1943]] in order to unify Tokyo City (東京市; Tōkyō-shi) and the existing Tokyo Prefecture (東京府; Tōkyō-fu) into the nation's capital. Currently, there are no defined city limits of Tokyo-shi, so Tokyo-shi and Tokyo-to are relatively synonymous.
'''Tokyo prefecture (東京都)'''

On [[March 20]] [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin]] [[nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system (in the tunnels beneath the political district of central Tokyo) in which 12 people were killed and thousands affected. ==Tokyo today== The city today is one of the world's most important urban centres. It is a major business and financial centre as well as the political capital of Japan. The city is unusual in that it has far fewer skyscrapers than other cities of its size, mostly due to earthquake construction codes. Rather it is mostly comprised of either low-rise apartments of six to ten floors or of densely packed family homes. Tokyo is also home to the world's most complex transit/train system and is world-famous for its crowded rush hours. == Geography == Tokyo prefecture is divided into mainland and island areas. The mainland is located at the northwest of [[Tokyo Bay]], about 90 km east to west and 25 km north to south. It borders [[Chiba prefecture]] in the east, [[Yamanashi prefecture]] in the west, [[Kanagawa prefecture]] in the south, and [[Saitama prefecture]] in the north. The islands are made up of [[Izu Islands]] and [[Ogasawara Islands]], stretching 1,000km in the [[Pacific Ocean]].

''A simulated-color satellite image of Tokyo, Japan, taken on [[NASA]]'s [[Landsat 7]] satellite.''
===Wards=== Tokyo prefecture has [[23 special wards]] in an area of about 621 square kilometers. As of September 1, 2002 the total population of the 23 wards was about 8.28 million, with a population density of 13,333 persons per square kilometer. [[de:Tokio]] [[eo:Tokio]] [[es:Tokio]] [[fr:Tokyo]] [[ja:東京都]] [[nl:Tokio]] [[pl:Tōkyō]] [[pt:Tóquio]] [[zh:东京]]
[[Image:tokyotower.jpg|tokyotower.jpg]]
''[[Tokyo Tower]] and [[Shiba Park]]''
'''Tōkyō''' (東京; lit. eastern capital) is the capital and largest city of [[Japan]], and the most populous [[metropolitan area]] in the world. A little more than 12 million people live in the city while hundreds of thousands of others commute everyday from surrounding areas to work and do business in the city. Tokyo is the business center of the country as well as the home of the [[Japanese emperor]] and the seat of the national government. The city is well known for its highly modern skyscrapers, thousands of flashing neon signs, a bustling network of roads always filled with traffic, and a very extensive [[metro|underground railway system]]. Tokyo occupies the '''Tokyo prefecture''' (東京都; Tōkyō-to, Tokyo prefecture), which is located in the [[Kanto region]] on [[Honshu]] [[island]]. The prefecture is sometimes referred to as the ''Tokyo Metropolitan Prefecture'' or ''Tokyo Metropolitan Area''. This stems from the fact that the character used for ''prefecture'' for Tokyo, which can also be read as ''capital'' or ''metropolis'', differs from the character used for other prefectures. Tokyo is often considered part of the [[Greater Tokyo Area]], which consists of Tokyo prefecture itself and the surrounding prefectures of [[Kanagawa]], [[Saitama]] and [[Chiba]]. The Greater Tokyo area is the largest metropolitan area in the world with a population of 33,418,366. The government's name of prefecture and city is '''Tokyo Metropolitan Government'''. The offices are located in the ward of [[Shinjuku]]. While there is no municipality called Tokyo, the old Tokyo city that existed until [[1943]], covering the area of the present [[23 special wards]], is still regarded as the single largest city and the [[capital]] of [[Japan]] with population of 8,134,688 and area of 621.3 km2. This designation was made in [[1943]] in order to unify Tokyo City (東京市; Tōkyō-shi) and the existing Tokyo Prefecture (東京府; Tōkyō-fu) into the nation's capital. Currently, there are no defined city limits of Tokyo-shi, so Tokyo-shi and Tokyo-to are relatively synonymous.
'''Tokyo prefecture (東京都)'''

According to the Population Census in 2000, Tokyo has population of 12,064,101 and area of 2186.9 km2. Tokyo literally means "eastern capital" in [[Japanese language|Japanese]], to mean to oppose to an old capital in west, [[Kyoto]], which was renamed "Saikyo", meaning "western capital", for a period of time. It was previously alternatively spelled '''Tokio''' in [[English language|English]], and is still spelled Tokio in some other languages like [[Dutch language|Dutch]], [[Esperanto]], [[German language|German]], and [[Spanish language|Spanish]]. ==History== Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], the city was known as '''[[Edo]]''' (江戸). The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[shogunate]] was established in [[1603]] with Edo as its seat of government (de facto capital). (The emperor's residence, and formal capital, remained in [[Kyoto]], that city had been the actual capital of Japan until that time.) In [[1868]], when the shogunate came to an end, the city was renamed "Tokyo" which means "Eastern Capital"; during the restoration, the emperor moved to Tokyo, making the city the formal as well as de facto capital of Japan. A major [[earthquake]] struck Tokyo in [[1923]], killing approximately 70,000 people; a massive reconstruction plan was drawn up, but was too expensive to carry out except in part. Despite this, the city grew until the beginning of [[World War II]]. During the war, Tokyo was [[Bombing of Tokyo in World War II|heavily bombed]], much of the city was burned to the ground, and its population in [[1945]] was only half that of [[1940]]. [[Douglas MacArthur|General Douglas MacArthur]] established his Occupation headquarters in what is now the Dai-Ichi Seimei building overlooking the Imperial Palace and, in the post-war years, and especially stimulated by the [[Korean War]], Japan experienced an economic miracle that led it from post-war deprivation to tremendous economic success. In the process, Japan entered and very often came to dominate a range of industries including steel, shipbuilding, automobiles, semi-conductors, consumer electronics. Although the [[recession]] following the bursting of the "bubble economy" in the early [[1990s]] hurt the city, Tokyo has become one of the most dynamic capital cities on earth. It has a tremendous range of social and economic activities, with myriad restaurants and clubs, a major financial district, tremendous industrial strength, a wealth of shops and entertainment opportunities. The investment boom of the late [[1980s]] is perhaps the greatest the world has ever known (as judged e.g. by the level of building expenditures in relation to the size of the economy) and, as a result, Tokyo has an enormously more modern capital stock (of buildings) than, e.g., [[London]] or [[New York City]].

On [[March 20]] [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin]] [[nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system (in the tunnels beneath the political district of central Tokyo) in which 12 people were killed and thousands affected (see [[Sarin gas attack on the Tokyo subway]]). ==Tokyo today== The city today is one of the world's most important urban centres. It is a major business and financial centre as well as the political capital of Japan. The city is unusual in that it has far fewer skyscrapers than other cities of its size, mostly due to earthquake construction codes. Rather it is mostly comprised of either low-rise apartments of six to ten floors or of densely packed family homes. Tokyo is also home to the world's most complex transit/train system and is world-famous for its crowded rush hours. == Geography == Tokyo prefecture is divided into mainland and island areas. The mainland is located at the northwest of [[Tokyo Bay]], about 90 km east to west and 25 km north to south. It borders [[Chiba prefecture]] in the east, [[Yamanashi prefecture]] in the west, [[Kanagawa prefecture]] in the south, and [[Saitama prefecture]] in the north. The islands are made up of [[Izu Islands]] and [[Ogasawara Islands]], stretching 1,000km in the [[Pacific Ocean]]. ===Wards===

On [[March 20]] [[1995]] the city became the focus of international media attention in the wake of the [[Aum Shinrikyo]] cult terrorist organisation attack with [[Sarin]] [[nerve gas]] on the Tokyo subway system (in the tunnels beneath the political district of central Tokyo) in which 12 people were killed and thousands affected (see [[Sarin gas attack on the Tokyo subway]]). ==Tokyo today== The city today is one of the world's most important urban centres. It is a major business and financial centre as well as the political capital of Japan. The city is unusual in that it has far fewer skyscrapers than other cities of its size, mostly due to earthquake construction codes. Rather it is mostly comprised of either low-rise apartments of six to ten floors or of densely packed family homes. Tokyo is also home to the world's most complex transit/train system and is world-famous for its crowded rush hours. == Geography == Tokyo prefecture is divided into mainland and island areas. The mainland is located at the northwest of [[Tokyo Bay]], about 90 km east to west and 25 km north to south. It borders [[Chiba prefecture]] in the east, [[Yamanashi prefecture]] in the west, [[Kanagawa prefecture]] in the south, and [[Saitama prefecture]] in the north. The islands are made up of [[Izu Islands]] and [[Ogasawara Islands]], stretching 1,000km in the [[Pacific Ocean]]. ===Wards===

taken by [[Landsat 7]]''
[[media:Large_Tokyo_Landsat.jpg|Larger version

Tokyo prefecture has [[23 special wards]] in an area of about 621 square kilometers. As of September 1, 2002 the total population of the 23 wards was about 8.28 million, with a population density of 13,333 persons per square kilometer. [[de:Tokio]] [[eo:Tokio]] [[es:Tokio]] [[fr:Tokyo]] [[ja:東京都]] [[nl:Tokio]] [[pl:Tōkyō]] [[pt:Tóquio]] [[zh:东京]]
[[Image:tokyotower.jpg|tokyotower.jpg]]
''[[Tokyo Tower]] and [[Shiba Park]]''
'''Tōkyō''' (東京; lit. eastern capital) is the capital and largest city of [[Japan]], and the most populous [[metropolitan area]] in the world. A little more than 12 million people live in the city while hundreds of thousands of others commute everyday from surrounding areas to work and do business in the city. Tokyo is the business center of the country as well as the home of the [[Japanese emperor]] and the seat of the national government. The city is well known for its highly modern skyscrapers, thousands of flashing neon signs, a bustling network of roads always filled with traffic, and a very extensive and complex [[metro|underground railway system]]. Administratively, Tokyo is a "metropolis" (都 ''to''), and the '''Tokyo Metropolitan Government''' has administrative authority over the [[23 special wards]] comprising the inner city of Tokyo, with a combined population of 8,134,688 and area of 621.3 km2. The Metropolitan Government also administers twenty-six outlying suburbs to the west (classified as cities) and a number of small islands in the [[Pacific Ocean]] (see below). The offices are located in the ward of [[Shinjuku]]. While there is no municipality called Tokyo, the old [[Tokyo City]] that existed until [[1943]], covering the area of the present [[23 special wards]]. Tokyo is also part of the [[Greater Tokyo Area]], which consists of Tokyo prefecture itself and the surrounding prefectures of [[Kanagawa]], [[Saitama]] and [[Chiba]]. The Greater Tokyo area is the largest metropolitan area in the world with a population of 33,418,366.

According to the Population Census in 2000, Tokyo has population of 12,064,101 and area of 2186.9 km2. Tokyo literally means "eastern capital" in [[Japanese language|Japanese]], to mean to oppose to an old capital in west, [[Kyoto]], which was renamed "Saikyo", meaning "western capital", for a period of time. It was previously alternatively spelled '''Tokio''' in [[English language|English]], and is still spelled Tokio in some other languages like [[Dutch language|Dutch]], [[Esperanto]], [[German language|German]], and [[Spanish language|Spanish]]. ==History== Before the [[Meiji Restoration]], the city was known as '''[[Edo]]''' (江戸). The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[shogunate]] was established in [[1603]] with Edo as its seat of government (de facto capital). (The emperor's residence, and formal capital, remained in [[Kyoto]], that city had been the actual capital of Japan until that time.) In [[1868]], when the shogunate came to an end, the city was renamed "Tokyo" which means "Eastern Capital"; during the restoration, the emperor moved to Tokyo, making the city the formal as well as de facto capital of Japan. A major [[earthquake]] struck Tokyo in [[1923]], killing approximately 70,000 people; a massive reconstruction plan was drawn up, but was too expensive to carry out except in part. Despite this, the city grew until the beginning of [[World War II]]. During the war, Tokyo was [[Bombing of Tokyo in World War II|heavily bombed]], much of the city was burned to the ground, and its population in [[1945]] was only half that of [[1940]]. [[Douglas MacArthur|General Douglas MacArthur]] established his Occupation headquarters in what is now the Dai-Ichi Seimei building overlooking the Imperial Palace and, in the post-war years, and especially stimulated by the [[Korean War]], Japan experienced an economic miracle that led it from post-war deprivation to tremendous economic success. In the process, Japan entered and very often came to dominate a range of industries including steel, shipbuilding, automobiles, semi-conductors, consumer electronics. Although the [[recession]] following the bursting of the "bubble economy" in the early [[1990s]] hurt the city, Tokyo has become one of the most dynamic capital cities on earth. It has a tremendous range of social and economic activities, with myriad restaurants and clubs, a major financial district, tremendous industrial strength, a wealth of shops and entertainment opportunities. The investment boom of the late [[1980s]] is perhaps the greatest the world has ever known (as judged e.g. by the level of building expenditures in relation to the size of the economy) and, as a result, Tokyo has an enormously more modern capital stock (of buildings) than, e.g., [[London]] or [[New York City]].

It borders [[Chiba prefecture]] in the east, [[Yamanashi prefecture]] in the west, [[Kanagawa prefecture]] in the south, and [[Saitama prefecture]] in the north. The islands are made up of [[Izu Islands]] and [[Ogasawara Islands]], stretching 1,000km in the [[Pacific Ocean]]. ===Wards===

'''Tōkyō''' (東京; lit. eastern capital) is the capital and largest city of [[Japan]], and the most populous [[metropolitan area]] in the world. A little more than 12 million people live in the city while hundreds of thousands of others commute everyday from surrounding areas to work and do business in the city. Tokyo is the central place of politics, economy, culture and academics in Japan as well as the home of the [[Japanese emperor]] and the seat of the national government. The city is well known for its highly modern skyscrapers, thousands of flashing neon signs, a bustling network of roads always filled with traffic, and a very extensive and complex [[metro|underground railway system]]. Tokyo literally means "eastern capital" in [[Japanese language|Japanese]], to mean to oppose to an old capital in west, [[Kyoto]], which was renamed "Saikyo", meaning "western capital", for a period of time. It was previously alternatively spelled '''Tokio''' in [[English language|English]], and is still spelled Tokio in some other languages like [[Dutch language|Dutch]], [[Esperanto]], [[German language|German]], and [[Spanish language|Spanish]]. == Administration ==
